title: The temptation of Jesus part 7
scripture_text: ‘’Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry. The tempter came to him and said, “If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.” Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.’” Then the devil took him to the holy city and had him stand on the highest point of the temple. “If you are the Son of God, he said, “throw yourself down. For it is written: “‘He will command his angels concerning you, and they will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone.’ Jesus answered him, ‘’It is also written: ‘Do not put the Lord your God to the test.’ ’Again, the devil took him to a very high mountain and showed him all the kingdoms of the world and their splendour “All this I will give you,” he said, “if you will bow down and worship me.” ‘Jesus said to him, “Away from me, Satan! For it is written: ‘Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only.’ Then the devil left him, and angels came and attended him.
devotional:
The second temptation.

We must remember that when it comes to temptation, things are never as they seem, behind every temptation there is a hidden agenda, a devious subplot, an evil intention. We also need to remember that the goal of the evil one is to rob us of our identity; he wants us to cease to live as children of God.

On the surface this may not appear to be a serious temptation. I have never been tempted to climb up to the top of a tall building and jump, it has never crossed my mind. However, if we dig a bit deeper we will discover how subtle this temptation is. To help us understand this temptation let me mention a Jewish tradition that said that the Messiah would manifest himself by leaping from the pinnacle of the temple. Satan was tempting Jesus to make a name for himself. ‘Jesus do something spectacular and show the world how wonderful you really are.’

We live in a world were lonely, empty people long for fame. Fame is the saddest substitute for genuineness in our society. Fame is adulation: admiration without love and commitment. Fame is achieved by jumping off temple walls while the crowd watches; it is a cheap alternative to the respect that can only be earned by consistently showing up in people's lives with humility, honestly and with compassion. In our culture fame comes with being the most outrageous. Because people crave attention and love, they pursue fame and notoriety in order to fulfil the haunting emptiness they feel inside.

God’s work in our lives is more often a process than a spectacular event.

Remember the intention of Satan is to rob a person of their identity. When reputation becomes more important than identity then we will cease to be ourselves, and become the person others expect us to be. When reputation become more important than identity then will feel a greater need to jump off pinnacles, or jump through hoops, or do anything else we can, in order to be noticed or appreciated. The security that children of God should be experiencing will protect them from needing the applause of the public. We have all been there at some stage in our lives, feeling that we must do certain things, or be a certain kind of person in order to prove ourselves so we will be accepted. This temptation is anti-grace, because it basis acceptance on performance.

But I think there is an even more serious intention behind this second temptation. Satan wants to change how we relate to God. He wants us to view God as more of a ‘fixer’, than a father. Jesus you jump and God will deal with the consequences. Often as Christians we make unwise decisions, we make foolish choices, and we come to God with a demanding attitude, expecting him to clean up our mess. We don’t engage with God as a child would a loving, caring father, but we see him as someone is to keep our lives sweet and easy, like a genie in the bottle.

God takes His relationship with you far too seriously for you just to use him and view him as a fixer. God is not our lives janitor responsible for clearing up after us. Viewing God this way will diminish our ability to relate to him as his Children. Approaching God merely as a ‘fixer,’ or deliverer will prevent any desire or ability to cultivate intimacy with him.

Satan will always encourage us not to take responsibility for our self, and in turn this will cause us to become demanding of God, we will think in terms of God having to do certain things to prove his love. God if you love me you will catch me when I jump. If we get caught in this temptation Satan will manage to trivialise our relationship with God. Being responsible is one of the ways we protect our identities as the children of God. So instead of blaming others for the mess of our lives, and expecting God to clean up the mess, we need to take full responsibility for our own lives and the condition of our hearts.

Satan wanted Jesus the seek vindication outside the purposes of God. Satan is saying, ‘OK Jesus let’s see how dependant you really are on your father, jump, take a dive and authenticate your claim.’

There is something in all of us that wants, to be vindicated; we want others to see that we are right. Often our identity and how we see ourselves are so entwined with our needing to be seen as being right.

As children of God, you don’t need to prove yourself. Your identity, value or significance has nothing whatsoever to do with how others see you or think about you.

Satan misuses the word of God, he quotes psalm 91:11-12. He uses the bible like a book of magic formula, and takes verses and misapplies them.

God has promised to care and protect us as we try to live for him in our normal everyday lives, however, when we become unwise, and begin to misuse the word of God to justify our foolish choices, we will have to live with the consequences.

How did Jesus resist this temptation?

Jesus once again quotes from the book of Deuteronomy. In chapter 6:16 it says, “Do not test the lord your God.” What is Jesus saying here? When Jesus quoted this verse he did not quote the last part of the verse. The whole verse reads “Do not test the Lord your God as you did in Massah. At Massah the people accused Moses and God of impotence and indifference. They said, “If you don’t take care of us as we expect, God, we are not going to follow you any more. You have to prove that you are God and that you care for us!” In other words, they challenged God’s power and His benevolence in spite of ample evidence to the contrary. Why did they do this? Because they believed that God should act according to their will. They demonstrated that their faith depended entirely on what God would do for them, not on who God was. The test at Massah is a sign of rebellious disobedience, a refusal to accept the character of God as the basis of obedience.

We have all been to Massah; we have all been in the place where we make demands of God, where we think he owes us something, where we made a contractual agreement with Him. ‘God if you do this for me I will follow you.’ We all are guilty of coming to God with a demanding attitude asking that he prove himself. However, this kind of thinking is a satanic trap, entered into through our refusal to respond spiritually to temptation.
prayer: Father thank you that you don’t need to prove yourself to us and that we don’t need to do the spectacular in order that you will love us and accept us.
html:
The temptation of Jesus part 7

Study Text: Matthew 4: 1-11
‘’Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry. The tempter came to him and said, “If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.” Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.’” Then the devil took him to the holy city and had him stand on the highest point of the temple. “If you are the Son of God, he said, “throw yourself down. For it is written: “‘He will command his angels concerning you, and they will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone.’ Jesus answered him, ‘’It is also written: ‘Do not put the Lord your God to the test.’ ’Again, the devil took him to a very high mountain and showed him all the kingdoms of the world and their splendour “All this I will give you,” he said, “if you will bow down and worship me.” ‘Jesus said to him, “Away from me, Satan! For it is written: ‘Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only.’ Then the devil left him, and angels came and attended him.

We have all been there at some stage in our lives, feeling that we must do certain things, or be a certain kind of person in order to prove ourselves so we will be accepted. This temptation is anti-grace, because it basis acceptance on performance.

But I think there is an even more serious intention behind this second temptation. Satan wants to change how we relate to God. He wants us to view God as more of a ‘fixer’, than a father. Jesus you jump and God will deal with the consequences. Often as Christians we make unwise decisions, we make foolish choices, and we come to God with a demanding attitude, expecting him to clean up our mess. We don’t engage with God as a child would a loving, caring father, but we see him as someone is to keep our lives sweet and easy, like a genie in the bottle.

God takes His relationship with you far too seriously for you just to use him and view him as a fixer. God is not our lives janitor responsible for clearing up after us. Viewing God this way will diminish our ability to relate to him as his Children. Approaching God merely as a ‘fixer,’ or deliverer will prevent any desire or ability to cultivate intimacy with him.

Satan will always encourage us not to take responsibility for our self, and in turn this will cause us to become demanding of God, we will think in terms of God having to do certain things to prove his love. God if you love me you will catch me when I jump. If we get caught in this temptation Satan will manage to trivialise our relationship with God. Being responsible is one of the ways we protect our identities as the children of God. So instead of blaming others for the mess of our lives, and expecting God to clean up the mess, we need to take full responsibility for our own lives and the condition of our hearts.

There is something in all of us that wants, to be vindicated; we want others to see that we are right. Often our identity and how we see ourselves are so entwined with our needing to be seen as being right.

As children of God, you don’t need to prove yourself. Your identity, value or significance has nothing whatsoever to do with how others see you or think about you.

How did Jesus resist this temptation?

Jesus once again quotes from the book of Deuteronomy. In chapter 6:16 it says, “Do not test the lord your God.” What is Jesus saying here? When Jesus quoted this verse he did not quote the last part of the verse. The whole verse reads “Do not test the Lord your God as you did in Massah. At Massah the people accused Moses and God of impotence and indifference. They said, “If you don’t take care of us as we expect, God, we are not going to follow you any more. You have to prove that you are God and that you care for us!” In other words, they challenged God’s power and His benevolence in spite of ample evidence to the contrary. Why did they do this? Because they believed that God should act according to their will. They demonstrated that their faith depended entirely on what God would do for them, not on who God was. The test at Massah is a sign of rebellious disobedience, a refusal to accept the character of God as the basis of obedience.

We have all been to Massah; we have all been in the place where we make demands of God, where we think he owes us something, where we made a contractual agreement with Him. ‘God if you do this for me I will follow you.’ We all are guilty of coming to God with a demanding attitude asking that he prove himself. However, this kind of thinking is a satanic trap, entered into through our refusal to respond spiritually to temptation.

Prayer: Father thank you that you don’t need to prove yourself to us and that we don’t need to do the spectacular in order that you will love us and accept us.
Bible in a year reading plan: 1 Kings 1-3
